theorem_title: Lean Eval: Perron-Frobenius positive eigenvector for irreducible nonnegative matrices
theorem_statement: Let A be a real square matrix indexed by a finite type n. If A is irreducible and nonnegative in the matrix sense, then A has a strictly positive real eigenvector for the real spectral radius.
raw_proof_text:
Let Delta be the standard simplex of nonnegative vectors whose coordinates sum to 1. For epsilon > 0, define A_epsilon = A + epsilon J, where J is the all-ones matrix. Then every entry of A_epsilon is strictly positive.

Define T_epsilon : Delta -> Delta by T_epsilon(x) = A_epsilon x divided by the sum of the coordinates of A_epsilon x. This is well-defined because A_epsilon x has strictly positive coordinates for x in Delta. It is continuous and maps the compact convex simplex Delta to itself. By Brouwer's fixed point theorem, T_epsilon has a fixed point x_epsilon in Delta. Equivalently, there is a scalar rho_epsilon > 0 such that A_epsilon x_epsilon = rho_epsilon x_epsilon. Because A_epsilon has strictly positive entries, every coordinate of x_epsilon is strictly positive.

Choose a sequence epsilon_m -> 0. By compactness of Delta, after passing to a subsequence we have x_epsilon_m -> x in Delta. The scalars rho_epsilon_m are bounded because rho_epsilon_m is the sum of the coordinates of A_epsilon_m x_epsilon_m and the entries of A_epsilon_m remain bounded. Passing to a further subsequence, rho_epsilon_m -> rho >= 0. Taking limits in A_epsilon_m x_epsilon_m = rho_epsilon_m x_epsilon_m gives A x = rho x. Since x is in Delta, x is nonzero.

We show rho > 0. If rho = 0, then A x = 0 and hence A^k x = 0 for every k >= 1. Since x is nonzero and nonnegative, choose j with x_j > 0. By irreducibility, for every index i there is k such that (A^k)_ij > 0. Then (A^k x)_i >= (A^k)_ij x_j > 0, contradicting A^k x = 0. Hence rho > 0.

We show x has strictly positive coordinates. Suppose x_i = 0. Since A x = rho x, we have (A x)_i = 0. This is a sum of nonnegative terms, so for every j with x_j > 0 we must have A_ij = 0. Applying the same reasoning to A^k x = rho^k x, we get (A^k x)_i = 0 for every k. Choose j with x_j > 0. Irreducibility gives k with (A^k)_ij > 0, and then (A^k x)_i >= (A^k)_ij x_j > 0, a contradiction. Thus every coordinate of x is strictly positive.

Finally, let lambda be any complex eigenvalue of A with nonzero eigenvector z. Let |z| denote the coordinatewise absolute value vector. Since A has nonnegative entries, |lambda| |z| = |lambda z| = |A z| <= A |z| coordinatewise. Because x has strictly positive coordinates, there is C > 0 such that |z| <= C x coordinatewise. Iterating gives |lambda|^m |z| <= A^m |z| <= C A^m x = C rho^m x. Choose i with |z_i| > 0. Then (|lambda|/rho)^m <= C x_i / |z_i| for all m, so |lambda| <= rho. Thus rho is the spectral radius and x is a strictly positive eigenvector.

Likely Formal Islands graph: perturb by epsilon J; use Brouwer on the simplex; pass to the limit; use irreducibility to show rho > 0 and positivity of the limiting eigenvector; prove spectral-radius domination. The best islands are the irreducibility-implies-positivity argument and the spectral-radius domination argument.
